network.overall_strategy: This network represents a literature-constrained prebiotic synthesis scenario for arginine in which the target remains chemically unresolved but is approached through the best-supported constructive branch: surface cyanosulfidic chemistry generating an arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor, followed by hydrolysis to arginine. A second major branch uses direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide, which is chemically validated but not demonstrated end-to-end from simple feedstocks under fully prebiotic conditions. A third, explicitly speculative branch follows a prebiotic urea-cycle analog through citrulline. Hydrothermal chemistry is limited to feedstock provisioning and mild vent-interface amino-acid-compatible conditions because the literature emphasizes arginine instability at hotter hydrothermal regimes. The network therefore integrates hydrothermal carbon/nitrogen provisioning, surface UV-driven cyanosulfidic construction, and biochemical/mineral concentration-preservation steps without overstating unsupported conversions.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent-ocean mixing interface, anoxic, H2-rich

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface CO2 hydrogenation on iron-sulfur phases yields reduced one-carbon products such as formate under mild vent-interface conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Used here strictly as hydrothermal feedstock provisioning rather than direct arginine synthesis. Mild alkaline vent interfaces are favored over hotter settings because arginine is thermally labile; energetic favorability of amino-acid-relevant synthesis is predicted near ~50C in alkaline hydrothermal systems, and greigite is a plausible protoferredoxin catalyst (Kitadai, 2015; Jordan et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical generation of cyanamide from reduced atmospheric mixtures

conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow pool, anaerobic, represented as environmentally compressed photochemical sourcing of cyanamide from reduced atmospheric cyanide/ammonia chemistry

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven reduced nitrogen-carbon photochemistry furnishes cyanamide as a prebiotic condensing and guanylating reagent.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanamide is strongly supported as prebiotically available from UV irradiation of reduced gas mixtures and is a central node in cyanosulfidic protometabolism; this node is included as an environmental source term because the literature summary supports cyanamide availability but does not provide a single fully resolved surface stoichiometry under the arginine-focused network constraints (Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin, 1965; Patel et al., 2015; Powner, Gerland & Sutherland, 2009).

reactions.name: Ammonium cyanide photochemistry to guanidine

conditions.catalyst: iron-assisted UV photochemistry on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: 254 nm UV, pH 8.4-8.5, anaerobic, prolonged irradiation from days to weeks

reactions.mechanism: Photolysis of ammonium cyanide solutions produces guanidine through cyanide-derived oligomer and rearrangement chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Guanidine was obtained in 8-20% yield from UV-irradiated ammonium cyanide solutions under mildly alkaline conditions with iron present; it is included as a side nitrogen-rich pool relevant to guanidinium chemistry, though not a demonstrated direct precursor attachment route to arginine (Lohrmann, 1972; Robertson & Miller, 1995).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic cross-coupling to acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system associated with sulfide mineral setting
conditions.other: 35C, UV irradiation, H2S present, phosphate-buffered, anaerobic

reactions.mechanism: Cu(I)-mediated photoredox coupling of HCN and acetylene in a cyanosulfidic network gives acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the first experimentally grounded step of the best-supported arginine-precursor route and must retain the documented cyanosulfidic context: HCN, H2S, Cu photoredox, phosphate buffer, UV light, 35C, anaerobic conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Acrylonitrile ammonolysis to beta-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, associated with H2S/Cu photochemical network

reactions.mechanism: Conjugate addition of ammonia to activated acrylonitrile affords beta-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This transformation is part of the Patel/Sutherland arginine-precursor branch and is retained with the same overall cyanosulfidic environmental regime rather than an invented alternative (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Photoreductive conversion of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of the nitrile-derived precursor leads to the corresponding amino aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research summary specifically identifies reduction of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde within the cyanosulfidic route; the exact micro-mechanism remains embedded in the Patel/Tsanakopoulou network rather than independently redefined here (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde by cyanamide

conditions.catalyst: surface concentration on clay within cyanosulfidic pool
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, UV-associated surface pool; especially efficient according to cyanosulfidic study

reactions.mechanism: Primary amine attack on cyanamide installs a guanyl functionality onto the amino aldehyde precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The especially efficient reaction of beta-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide is explicitly highlighted as the key guanidinium-installation event leading toward arginine precursor 46. Montmorillonite is included as a plausible concentrating mineral, but the core chemical justification follows the cyanosulfidic literature (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017; Catalano et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoreduction and cyanide addition to arginine aminonitrile precursor

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox in cyanosulfidic surface setting

reactions.mechanism: Further photoreductive homologation and cyanide incorporation convert the guanylated aldehyde branch into the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This step corresponds to the reported progression to compound 46, described as the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine in the cyanosulfidic protometabolism network. Quantitative yields were not reported, so the node is literature-supported but not kinetically resolved (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine aminonitrile precursor to arginine

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis during pool aging; kept below destructive hydrothermal temperatures

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis converts the alpha-aminonitrile precursor into the amino acid arginine.
reactions.reasoning: The hydrolysis of compound 46 to arginine is the final implied step of the Patel/Sutherland route, but a continuous end-to-end experimental demonstration from simple feedstocks to isolated arginine has not been achieved; this node therefore reflects the literature's most plausible completion step without claiming full experimental closure (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017; Miller & Orgel, 1974).

reactions.name: Direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide

conditions.catalyst: mineral-surface concentration; non-prebiotic high-yield aqueous catalysis known separately
conditions.other: direct guanylation conceptually valid in water; classic base-promoted chemistry known, and modern aqueous catalysis reaches high yield at 100C but with non-prebiotic scandium triflate

reactions.mechanism: The delta-amino group of ornithine attacks cyanamide, generating the guanidinium side chain of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the classic chemically substantiated route to arginine: ornithine plus cyanamide. It was first demonstrated with Ba(OH)2 and later shown to proceed efficiently in water with scandium triflate at 100C, but not in a complete prebiotic continuous experiment. Here it is retained as a literature-essential branch while explicitly acknowledging incomplete prebiotic validation (Schulze & Winterstein, 1899; Tsubokura et al., 2014; Johnson et al., 2008; Meierhenrich et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Ornithine carbamoylation by cyanate to citrulline

conditions.other: anoxic pool chemistry; hypothetical prebiotic urea-cycle analog

reactions.mechanism: Terminal amino carbamoylation of ornithine by cyanate furnishes citrulline.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction captures the hypothetical prebiotic urea-cycle analog discussed in the literature, where cyanate substitutes for carbamoyl phosphate. It is included as an explicitly speculative but literature-motivated branch and not as a validated route (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Speculative citrulline-to-arginine conversion using cyanamide

conditions.other: hypothetical low-temperature pool chemistry; no direct experimental demonstration

reactions.mechanism: Conceptual guanyl-transfer/rearrangement step within a prebiotic urea-cycle analog leading from citrulline to arginine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature summary identifies a prebiotic urea-cycle analog as entirely hypothetical and does not provide an experimentally demonstrated citrulline-to-arginine prebiotic reaction. This node is therefore explicitly speculative and included only to represent the discussed open hypothesis (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021; Cunin et al., 1986).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer products to cyanate pool

conditions.catalyst: UV-exposed shallow pool with subsequent aqueous hydrolysis
conditions.other: anoxic; represented as environmental generation of cyanate-bearing hydrolysate products from HCN oligomer chemistry

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolytic processing yields a cyanate-containing nitrogen-carbon pool.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanate is invoked in the literature as a plausible prebiotic carbamoyl surrogate, and HCN oligomer hydrolysis is the relevant environmental source class. This node is intentionally conservative and does not claim a single resolved stoichiometric conversion beyond the supported cyanate-bearing hydrolysate concept (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Surface provenance of ornithine from spark-discharge/meteoritic amino acid inventory

conditions.catalyst: concentration rather than validated catalytic conversion
conditions.other: explicitly low-confidence placeholder for documented availability of ornithine in prebiotic inventory; not a mechanistically demonstrated conversion from glutamate

reactions.mechanism: Network connector representing the documented co-occurrence of glutamate-rich amino-acid inventories with trace ornithine in prebiotic experiments and extraterrestrial materials, without asserting a proven direct transformation.
reactions.reasoning: The literature clearly supports glutamate as prebiotically abundant and ornithine as detected in Miller volcanic discharge experiments and tentatively in Murchison, but does not provide a validated prebiotic glutamate-to-ornithine mechanism. This reaction is therefore an explicit connector node for inventory continuity and is marked as speculative by necessity (Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011; Meierhenrich et al., 2004; Naraoka et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite adsorption and protection of arginine

conditions.other: pH 5-7 interlayer adsorption in clay-rich pools

reactions.mechanism: Cation exchange selectively captures protonated arginine in montmorillonite interlayers, increasing retention and protection.
reactions.reasoning: Montmorillonite selectively adsorbs cationic amino acids, especially arginine and lysine, far more strongly than neutral amino acids, plausibly protecting arginine from UV and thermal loss once formed. This is a preservation step, not a synthetic proof (Catalano et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Partial hydrolysis branch of arginine aminonitrile precursor
reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydrolysis gives a partially hydrolyzed amide/acid precursor en route to arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis is generally stepwise; this branch is included to create a chemically realistic alternative completion route from compound 46 without inventing a new constructive pathway. It is a mechanistic elaboration of the Patel/Tsanakopoulou endpoint and remains inferential rather than directly isolated for arginine in the cited work (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Completion of partial hydrolysis intermediate to arginine

conditions.other: continued mild hydrolysis below destructive hydrothermal temperatures

reactions.mechanism: Further hydrolysis completes conversion of the precursor intermediate to arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Included as the second half of a stepwise hydrolysis completion route for the cyanosulfidic precursor, consistent with standard aminonitrile chemistry but not independently demonstrated for arginine in a continuous prebiotic experiment. The unresolved status of arginine synthesis is therefore preserved (Patel et al., 2015; Miller & Orgel, 1974).

reactions.name: Trace supercritical CO2/H2O arginine formation from pyruvic-acid-class feedstocks

conditions.other: trace side-path only; modeled to reflect supercritical CO2/H2O report, not a robust route; mechanism unresolved

reactions.mechanism: Poorly resolved multicomponent hydrothermal chemistry affords trace arginine under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions after hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: This branch is included only as a low-confidence literature side-path because Fujioka et al. reported trace arginine after acid hydrolysis under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions, but with unresolved mechanism and advanced starting materials. It should not be weighted comparably to the cyanosulfidic branch, and hotter hydrothermal settings are avoided because arginine is unstable there (Fujioka et al., 2009; Körner et al., 2021; Miller & Bada, 1988).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic arginine precursor route
pathways.description: Best-supported constructive route from HCN and acetylene through acrylonitrile, beta-aminopropionitrile, beta-aminopropionaldehyde, cyanamide-assisted guanylation, and hydrolysis of compound 46 to arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with stepwise hydrolysis completion
pathways.description: Same surface constructive branch as P1 but with two-step aminonitrile hydrolysis through a partially hydrolyzed intermediate before arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface-fed canonical route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon reduction provides reduced feedstock context that feeds into the surface cyanosulfidic route culminating in arginine.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine-cyanamide guanylation route
pathways.description: Two-phase strategy in which ornithine availability is assumed from documented prebiotic inventories and cyanamide directly installs the guanidinium group.

pathways.name: Direct guanylation followed by clay capture
pathways.description: Arginine formed by direct ornithine guanylation is immediately concentrated and protected by montmorillonite.

pathways.name: HCN-to-cyanate prebiotic urea-cycle analog
pathways.description: Speculative branch where HCN oligomer hydrolysis furnishes cyanate, ornithine is carbamoylated to citrulline, and citrulline is converted to arginine by cyanamide chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-fed urea-cycle analog
pathways.description: Hydrothermal feedstock provisioning and surface cyanamide sourcing support a speculative cyanate/citrulline route to arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanamide-rich cyanosulfidic route with independent guanidine pool
pathways.description: The main cyanosulfidic synthesis proceeds in a surface environment that also generates guanidine from ammonium cyanide, highlighting convergent guanidinium chemistry around cyanamide/HCN.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic route plus post-formation clay stabilization
pathways.description: The best-supported arginine-forming branch is followed by selective montmorillonite adsorption to improve persistence of the thermally labile product.

pathways.name: Trace supercritical side-route to arginine
pathways.description: Very low-confidence hydrothermal side branch included solely because trace arginine was reported under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions, despite unresolved mechanism and literature caution.
